title: Confirmatory Factor Analysis on Mental Health Status using ABCD Cohort
abstract: The general psychopathology factor (p factor), derived from a wide range of psychological symptoms, is proposed to approximate an individual’s tendency to develop a broad range of psychiatric disorders. The aim of this study was to extract the general p factor in a bifactor model from confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), as well as internalizing and externalizing factors, and test the stability of the factors derived from different behavioral measures. Using data from the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, we compared the latent factors derived from Child Behavior Checklist measure (a standard approach) with those from broader measures. Multiple linear regression was used to assess the relationship of the p factor with other behavioral or cognition measures and clinical diagnoses. The results showed that the p factor had greater stability and a higher correlation between models (r=0.87) than internalizing and externalizing factors (r=0.32,0.02, respectively). The p factor explained significant variances in all dimensional behavioral variables as well as neurocognition and was significantly related to all mental disorders available. A mixed-effects model was constructed to measure the association of the p factor with screen time activity, sleep duration, physical activity, household income, and gender (altogether, these explained 8.02% of the variance in the p factor). The findings from this study also show that the general p factor defined in the bifactor model has sufficient determinacy.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Paper reports only aggregate variance explained (8.02% for all predictors combined) with no individual coefficients for screen time extracted. Per B2 rule: summed/total effect reported, no per-item breakdown of SM coefficient separately.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: Variance explained by mixed-effects model = 8.02%; correlation coefficients r=0.87 (p factor models), r=0.32 (internalizing), r=0.02 (externalizing); CFA fit indices (CFI=0.957/0.948, TLI=0.928/0.927, RMSEA=0.112/0.060)

title: Problematic smartphone use: What can teenagers and parents do to reduce use?
abstract: In the study by Carter et al (2024), 657 16- to 18-year-olds were included from five schools in England and found an association between anxiety, depression and problematic smartphone use, a pattern of behaviour which resembles a behavioural addiction.1 Qualitative data from a younger sample revealed that while teenagers valued their smartphones for school and connection with family and friends, they recognised that smartphones impacted time spent on school work, with family and friends, and pursuing hobbies.2 So what to do? The good news is that teenagers are already trying to limit their smartphone use: further findings from the same sample but not reported in Carter et al 2024 were that 625 (95%) had used at least one smartphone reduction strategy. The most popular strategies were putting the smartphone on Silent (78.1%, n = 513), turning off the notifications (74.1%, n = 487) and using âDo not disturbâ or âAirplaneâ mode (67.9%, n = 446). (Supplementary Table S1) The strategies rated the most effective were: âDo not disturbâ (73.5%, n = 328/446); turning off the notifications (65.7%, n = 320/487); and leaving the smartphone in another room at bedtime (58.8%, n = 130/221). The least effective were those that restricted access to specific Apps (38.6%, n = 97/251); using a locked box during revision (36.0%, n = 27/75); and turning on greyscale (32.0%, n = 32/100). Participants also rated parents taking the Smartphone away at night (37.5%, n = 62/165) as least effective, which may reflect that this was imposed rather than a shared plan. We have previously surveyed a large study of 18- to 24-year-olds and found the same strategies were effective.3 The teenagers' perception that preventing notificationsâvia switching them off or putting the phone in silent was effective is supported by evidence.4 In a study of children and young people, they received a median of 237 notifications per day.5 The impact of notifications has been shown to reduce cognitive performance, and a randomised controlled trial found removing notifications reduced anxiety and depression.4 A contributing factor may be poor sleep caused by smartphone use at night, which has been suggested to blur the distinction between day and night due to continued use into the night.5 What can parents or carers do? Although the parental impulse may be to remove the smartphone entirely, there is scant evidence to support this. Restrictions have been found to raise family tension between parents and adolescents and was counter-productive.6 Rather, the relationship between parent and adolescent is key: Fostering a partnership where the harms and benefits to parents and adolescents are openly discussed was more likely to be effective as it acknowledges both adolescents' drive to individuation and their relative inexperience. Beyond the family, local norms in communities, schools and peer groups are likely to influence usage. Legislation is being used to influence this, for example, there is a French law outlining when employees can and cannot send emails to employees. Similar strategies may be effective if communities of parents and adolescents came together to reduce the feeling of missing out. Guidance may include families to not use devices at meal times and agree time at night to collectively stop using them. A further pressing need is to reduce access to harmful content, unfortunately there is little evidence of effective interventions. On the one hand, the smartphone may act as a social leveller allowing differing socioeconomic groups access to the same content (e.g., educational material), but in some likely to suffer negative consequences. The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study found differences in usage patterns across genders and age with minoritised groups exhibiting increased prevalence of video game and social media addiction.7 One of the thorny problems inherent in school-level public health interventions for problematic smartphone usage is balancing the benefit of inclusion with the potential for harm. Practically, a risk of reducing smartphone use, particularly social media use, is of social exclusion in a peer group where much social engagement and interchange occurs via smartphones. This âcollective action problemâ is vividly conveyed by a microeconomic study where university students who used social media platforms stated that they would require a median payment of $30 to give up TikTok or Instagram for a month, but were willing to pay a median of $10 if they and others gave it up.8 Thus, to tackle this a broader change in social norms may be necessary and studies on social strategies need to be prioritised. There are particular groups whose relationship with smartphones warrant special attention, such as neurodiverse populations. For example, there appears a link between worsening symptoms of ADHD and frequent notifications.9 Success of generic reduction strategies should be studied in this group, and parents given early a

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Article discusses smartphone use and reduction strategies but does not conduct original quantitative SM/phone analysis.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: While the article discusses smartphone use and reduction strategies, it is not an original empirical study. It is a perspective piece commenting on other research. No original qualifying exposure variable measurement.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: Opinion piece; no inferential testing of associations presented.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: No original inferential test. This is a perspective/commentary with references to other studies' findings.

disagreed_criteria.c1_evidence: No original numeric data extractable from this paper.
disagreed_criteria.c2_evidence: While percentages are cited (e.g., 95% used reduction strategies, 78.1% used silent mode), these come from referenced studies, not original analysis reported in this article.
